did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used convolutional neural network (CNN) models to noninvasively classify skin cancers (basal cell carcinomas, squamous cell carcinomas, and melanomas) using optical coherence tomography (OCT) images. The CNN models analyzed intensity distribution and pixel location to predict lesion types with sensitivities and specificities greater than 90%.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' in the header. It presents original experimental data, including a Methods section describing the collection of OCT images from 452 lesions (normal skin, BCC, SCC, melanoma), the development of convolutional neural network (CNN) models using ResNet18, and Results sections reporting specific sensitivity, specificity, and AUOC metrics for classifying skin cancers. The study involves primary data analysis and validation rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Clinical Use of Vibrational Coherence Tomography and Optical Coherence Tomography to Noninvasively Classify Skin Cancers: A New Telemedicine Technique
brief_summary: This study developed a noninvasive telemedicine technique to classify basal cell carcinomas (BCCs), squamous cell carcinomas (SCCs), and melanomas using optical coherence tomography (OCT) images. The researchers collected OCT images from 452 lesions and trained convolutional neural network (CNN) models based on the ResNet18 architecture to analyze intensity distribution and pixel location. The models were evaluated using a 5-fold cross-validation strategy, with performance assessed via sensitivity, specificity, and area under the operating curve (AUOC). The CNN models achieved sensitivities and specificities greater than 90% for distinguishing cancerous lesions from normal skin, with AUOC values ranging from 0.97 to 1.00. The results indicate that these AI models can rapidly classify skin cancers in several minutes, facilitating remote diagnosis in areas with limited dermatologist access.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied in 99 lesions; classified against normal skin using CNN models with 93.4% sensitivity and 100% specificity.
Studied in 47 lesions; classified against normal skin using CNN models with 94.0% sensitivity and 100% specificity.
Studied in 106 lesions; classified against normal skin using CNN models with 99.1% sensitivity and 100% specificity.
Studied in 200 lesions; used as the negative control class for binary classification against BCC, SCC, and melanoma.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Melanoma, and Normal Skin)
all_task_types.details: The study uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) based on ResNet18 to classify OCT images into discrete classes. The models perform binary classification tasks (e.g., Normal Skin vs. BCC, Normal Skin vs. SCC, Normal Skin vs. Melanoma) and multi-class classification. Performance is evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, and AUOC. While the model outputs probabilities for class prediction (e.g., Table 3 shows probabilities for BCC, SCC, and Melanoma), the primary task is categorical classification, not regression of a continuous variable.

datasets_used.name: Summit Health and Rutgers Center for Dermatology OCT Cohort
datasets_used.description: A private clinical dataset of Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT) images collected in vivo on intact skin and cancerous lesions as part of IRB-approved clinical studies at Summit Health (Berkeley Heights, NJ) and Rutgers Center for Dermatology (Somerset, NJ). Images were collected using the OptoScope device. Diagnoses were confirmed by board-certified dermatopathologists via H&E staining and review of tissue sections.

use.training: Used to train convolutional neural network (CNN) models based on the ResNet18 architecture. The dataset was split using a 5-fold cross-validation strategy.
use.validation: Used for validation during the 5-fold cross-validation process to select the best-performing model based on validation accuracy.
use.testing: Used for testing and performance evaluation (sensitivity, specificity, AUOC) via the 5-fold cross-validation strategy.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To ensure robust performance evaluation and select the best-performing model based on validation accuracy.
To assess the predictive power and clinical relevance of the CNN models for distinguishing cancerous lesions from normal skin.

statistical_methods_used.method: Area Under the Operating Curve (AUOC) / ROC-AUC analysis
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the overall discriminatory ability of the CNN models.
To visualize the classification results (true positives, false positives, etc.) for the CNN models.
Used as the loss function during the training of the convolutional neural network models.
Used to train the network over multiple epochs.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using sensitivity, specificity, and area under the operating curve (AUOC) via 5-fold cross-validation.

conclusions: Convolutional neural network (CNN) models can classify different skin cancers (basal cell carcinomas, squamous cell carcinomas, and melanomas) in several minutes based on optical coherence tomography (OCT) images.
The CNN models achieved sensitivities and specificities greater than 90% for distinguishing cancerous lesions from normal skin.
The models correctly classified BCC, SCC, and melanomas consistent with diagnoses based on lesion histopathology conducted by board-certified dermatopathologists.
Noninvasive use of the OptoScope provides digital information about skin cancers that can be used to classify skin cancers as part of an evaluation of patient lesions via telemedicine.

This technique is particularly useful in areas where dermatologists are in short supply and patient waiting times are prolonged, such as rural areas where dermatology visits are not readily available.
Evaluation using new digital techniques and the availability of approved topical treatments for these cancers will provide an effective manner to treat patients in rural areas.

The false positives observed were limited in number and did not lead to any missed classifications, although additional data collection is expected to improve sensitivity and specificity, particularly for SCCs.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, single-institution dataset (452 lesions total) collected from Summit Health and Rutgers Center for Dermatology, limiting generalizability and raising concerns about population bias.
The dataset is significantly imbalanced, with only 47 Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) lesions compared to 200 normal skin lesions, which may skew model performance metrics.

Performance metrics show potential inconsistencies: Table 2 reports 100% specificity for SCC and Melanoma, while the text describing Figure 6 notes that normal skin was classified as SCC 3 out of 50 times (implying 94% specificity), and Figure 7 caption claims 100% sensitivity/specificity for melanoma while Table 2 lists 99.1% sensitivity.
The paper does not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals, relying solely on sensitivity, specificity, and AUOC for evaluation.

Validation was performed using 5-fold cross-validation on a small dataset; there is no mention of an independent external validation cohort, which is a significant limitation for clinical deployment claims.

The CNN models were trained on grayscale OCT images converted to three-channel inputs using ImageJ color-coding, which may introduce artifacts or biases not present in standard medical imaging pipelines.
The study acknowledges that false positives were limited but does not provide detailed analysis of misclassified cases beyond the confusion matrix counts, limiting understanding of failure modes.

The clinical utility claim is based on a telemedicine workflow where images are collected by a trained assistant and analyzed remotely; however, the study does not assess inter-operator variability or the impact of assistant training on image quality.
